reviewed_by: AI Governance Lead, Contoso Health Insurance

gaps.notes: ISO 42001 has no dedicated safety-testing control. Safety considerations are distributed across A.6.2.4 (verification and validation) and A.5.4 (impact assessment) without a discrete safety subcategory.
ISO 42001 contains no explicit explainability or interpretability mandate. A.8.2 (system documentation) addresses transparency to users but not model-level explainability.
ISO 42001 contains no control on environmental impact of AI systems. A.5.5 (societal impacts) can be interpreted to include environmental concerns but does not require measurement.

gaps.source_title: Risk-tracking mechanisms at NIST subcategory granularity
gaps.notes: ISO 42001 treats risk tracking under Clause 6.1 (actions to address risks and opportunities) at the management-system level, not at the subcategory granularity NIST specifies in MEASURE 3.1-3.3.

ISO 42001 treats measurement feedback under Clause 9 (performance evaluation) at the management-system level. NIST subcategory-level feedback mechanisms have no direct equivalent in Annex A controls. See the paired Clause 9.2 to MEASURE 4.1, 4.2, 4.3 partial-match entries below for the clause-level relationship.

NIST AI RMF does not mandate a periodic AI policy review cadence. This is a management-system artifact inherited from the ISO 27001 structure. The closest analog is GOVERN 1.7 (decommissioning procedures), which is not equivalent.
NIST AI RMF contains no resource-provision subcategory. ISO 42001 treats resource identification and allocation as a distinct management-system requirement.
NIST AI RMF contains no dedicated tooling-resources subcategory. MAP 4.1 is complementary but scoped to third-party risk rather than internal tooling provision.
NIST AI RMF contains no compute-resources subcategory. ISO 42001 treats computing-capacity planning as a distinct management-system control.
NIST AI RMF documents controls but not the design process itself. ISO 42001 A.6.2.3 requires process-level documentation of design decisions that NIST subcategories do not specify.

gaps.source_title: Objectives for responsible use (distinct from A.6.1.2)
gaps.notes: NIST AI RMF conflates deployment and development objectives. ISO 42001 separates A.6.1.2 (development objectives) from A.9.3 (use objectives). NIST has no use-phase-specific objectives control.
EU AI Act prescribes internal-control and notified-body conformity assessment routes. ISO 42001 is a management-system certification and does not confer or replace EU conformity assessment.

gaps.source_title: CE marking and EU declaration of conformity
gaps.notes: CE marking and EU declaration of conformity are regulatory artefacts with no ISO management-system equivalent.
EU-level high-risk database registration is a public registry obligation absent from ISO 42001.
EU market-entry representative mandate has no ISO 42001 counterpart.
ISO 42001 has no incident-reporting deadline equivalent to Article 73 windows (15 days generic, 10 days widespread infringement, 2 days death).
EU Commission implementing-act template for PMM plans has no ISO 42001 equivalent.
ISO 42001 requires log retention proportionate to purpose but does not set the 6-month minimum floor of Article 19.
Fundamental-rights-specific methodologies (HUDERIA, Dutch FRAIA) referenced for Article 27 implementation have no ISO 42001 analog.
General-purpose AI systemic-risk regime (model evaluation, adversarial testing, incident tracking, cybersecurity protection) has no ISO 42001 equivalent.
EU GPAI copyright-policy requirement (including Article 4(3) CDSM opt-out) has no ISO 42001 analog.
GPAI public training-data summary via AI Office template has no ISO 42001 equivalent.
Real-world testing plan, informed consent, and market-surveillance authority registration have no ISO 42001 analog.

gaps.source_title: Market surveillance cooperation and compelled access
gaps.notes: Market surveillance authority cooperation duties and compelled source-code/training-data access have no ISO 42001 equivalent.
ISO 42001 is a voluntary management-system standard; it does not replicate the prohibition regime or its administrative fine tier (up to EUR 35 million or 7 percent turnover).
Real-time remote biometric identification derogation regime for law enforcement has no ISO 42001 analog.

EU declaration of conformity is a product-conformity attestation signed by the provider. ISO 42001 is a management-system standard and has no product-conformity equivalent. The eu-conformity-assessor plugin emits a TEMPLATE declaration the provider completes and signs.

CE marking is a regulatory product mark affixed under Union harmonisation legislation. ISO 42001 management-system certification confers no right to affix CE marking. The eu-conformity-assessor plugin checks marking location and notified-body identification but does not affix the mark.

EU-level public database registration of high-risk systems is a regulatory transparency obligation administered by the Commission. ISO 42001 has no public-registry counterpart. The eu-conformity-assessor plugin captures the registration status and flags restricted visibility for Annex III Point 6 (law enforcement) entries.
ISO requires periodic AI policy review cadence. EU AI Act does not mandate a policy review cycle; Article 17 QMS obligations imply maintenance but no cadence.
ISO mandates internal concern/whistleblower channel. EU AI Act has Article 73 external incident reporting but no internal channel requirement.
ISO specifies resource controls across human, tooling, systems, data, and compute. EU Article 17(1)(l) is less granular and does not break out resource categories.
ISO requires documenting the impact-assessment process itself (methodology). EU AI Act Article 27 requires outputs (the FRIA) but not process-level documentation.
ISO requires setting responsible-use objectives. EU Article 26 imposes use obligations but does not require defined use-objectives.
ISO requires a managed intended-use declaration. EU Article 26(1) requires deployers to use per provider instructions but does not require a formal declaration artefact.
ISO requires managed supplier, customer, and third-party relationships across the AI value chain. EU Article 25 addresses value-chain responsibility allocation but has a narrower scope.

ISO management-system clause requires continual improvement as an ongoing discipline. EU AI Act Article 20 mandates corrective action on nonconformity but does not impose a continual-improvement obligation.
ISO 42001 does not require publication of a public URL for the AI system. ATRS 1.3 is jurisdiction-specific public transparency.
gaps.citation: UK Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard v2.1

gaps.source_ref: UK ATRS, Section 2.8 Public Sector Equality Duty
gaps.source_title: Equality Act 2010 s.149 integration in impact assessment
gaps.notes: ATRS 2.8 expects documentation of Public Sector Equality Duty compliance under Equality Act 2010 s.149. ISO 42001 is jurisdiction-agnostic and does not reference UK statutory equality duties.

gaps.source_ref: UK ATRS, Section 4.1 Senior Responsible Owner
gaps.notes: ATRS 4.1 expects a named Senior Responsible Owner consistent with UK GovS 005. ISO Clause 5.3 requires defined roles but does not require the SRO title or equivalent UK civil-service accountability construct.
gaps.citation: UK Government Functional Standard GovS 005: Digital

gaps.source_ref: UK ATRS, Section Tier 1 public transparency default
gaps.notes: ATRS Tier 1 is a public-registry default mandated by CDDO in December 2023. ISO A.9.3 requires external communication with interested parties but does not require publication to a public registry.

gaps.source_ref: UK ATRS, Section 2.1 Data Ethics Framework
gaps.source_title: Data Ethics Framework and ETAF cross-references
gaps.notes: ATRS 2.1 expects ethics reasoning referencing UK Data Ethics Framework and ETAF. ISO 42001 references generic AI ethics principles but not these UK-specific frameworks.

gaps.source_ref: UK ATRS, Section 2.8 DPIA under UK GDPR
gaps.notes: ATRS 2.8 expects a DPIA in the form anticipated by UK GDPR and ICO guidance. ISO 42001 defers privacy management to ISO 27701 and does not prescribe DPIA format under UK DPA 2018.

ISO 42001 Clauses 4 to 10 require an organisation-wide management system (context, leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation, improvement). ATRS documents a single tool and does not constitute an AIMS.
ISO Clause 9.1 and the NIST MEASURE function both require ongoing measurement with defined metrics. ATRS Tier 1 does not require continuous measurement metrics as a published artefact.
ISO A.10.3 expects documented supplier controls including audit rights. ATRS 4.5 asks for supplier name and procurement route but does not require audit-right documentation.
ISO A.6.2.2 and the NIST GenAI Profile anticipate model, data, and system cards with defined depth. ATRS Tier 1 fields are shorter-form and do not mandate full model-card depth.

ISO Clause 8 and the NIST AI RMF require AI lifecycle-stage mapping (design, development, deployment, operation, decommissioning). ATRS treats the tool as a static published artefact without lifecycle-stage labelling.
ISO 42001 Clauses 4 to 10 require an organisation-wide management system. ATRS documents a single tool and does not constitute an AIMS.
ISO A.10.3 expects documented supplier audit rights. ATRS 4.5 asks for supplier name and procurement route but does not require audit-right documentation.

gaps.source_title: AG exclusive enforcement; no private right of action
gaps.notes: ISO 42001 is a voluntary management system standard with no public enforcement regime; Colorado enforcement allocation has no standard analog.
ISO 42001 applies regardless of deployer size; no size-based exemption exists.
